threats.name: Spoofing
threats.description: An adversary impersonates a trusted entity (ECU, user, or backend service) to gain unauthorized privileges or inject false data. This is the abstract base class for all spoofing threats.

threats.name: Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) Attack
threats.description: An attacker intercepts and alters communications between two entities without their knowledge, breaking secure channels.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Breaking or bypassing secure channels requires cryptographic or advanced network routing expertise.

equipment.rationale: Requires specialized interception hardware or CA compromise tools.

threats.name: Cloud/App Credential Spoofing
threats.description: An attacker impersonates a legitimate user by obtaining valid credentials (passwords, PINs, or static tokens), allowing unauthorized access to remote services.

threats.name: Malicious Firmware Update Deployment
threats.description: An attacker distributes and successfully installs tampered or unauthorized firmware packages by spoofing the backend or bypassing digital signature verifications.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires expert knowledge to bypass OEM signing structures or hardware roots of trust.

equipment.rationale: Requires specialized reverse engineering or glitching equipment.

threats.name: Counterfeit or Rogue Hardware Integration
threats.description: Exploitation of the supply chain or physical vehicle access to insert counterfeit or malicious hardware components that successfully bypass component authentication.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Requires confidential schematic or layout knowledge to successfully bypass hardware authentication.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires physical access to the vehicle internals or supply chain interdiction.

threats.name: Backend and Mobile App Spoofing
threats.description: An attacker forges information originating from remote external sources to deceive the vehicle into executing unauthorized commands.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Always available remotely if the vehicle has cellular coverage.

threats.name: In-Vehicle Network (IVN) Spoofing
threats.description: An attacker with access to the internal vehicle network generates or modifies messages to impersonate legitimate ECUs, bypassing internal trust boundaries.

threats.name: GNSS/Location Spoofing
threats.description: An attacker manipulates or falsifies the vehicle's reported location by generating stronger, counterfeit GNSS RF signals.

equipment.rationale: Requires SDR (Software Defined Radio) equipment which is highly accessible.

threats.name: ADAS Perception Spoofing
threats.description: An attacker projects false physical signals to trick the ADAS perception layer into detecting phantom objects or ignoring real ones.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Requires understanding of the specific sensor fusion logic to create effective adversarial inputs.

equipment.rationale: Requires specialized optical or EM projection hardware.

threats.name: Message Replay Attack
threats.description: Capturing valid network transmissions and retransmitting them later to trick the system into performing an unauthorized action.

threats.name: Diagnostic Credential Spoofing
threats.description: An attacker impersonates a technician by obtaining valid credentials (seeds, passwords), allowing unauthorized access to vehicle diagnostic functions.

threats.name: V2X Safety Message Injection
threats.description: An attacker broadcasts forged V2X Cooperative Awareness Messages (CAM) or Decentralized Environmental Notification Messages (DENM), spoofing phantom vehicles or false hazard conditions to manipulate ADAS decision logic or mislead the driver.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: V2X message formats (ETSI ITS) are public standards.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires proximity (DSRC ~300 m) or a rogue V2X RSU.

equipment.rationale: Requires a V2X-capable radio and a valid or forged pseudonym certificate.

threats.name: EV Charging Session Spoofing
threats.description: An attacker deploys a rogue or tampered EVSE that presents a forged TLS certificate during the ISO 15118 Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) handshake. Upon a successful TLS downgrade or certificate validation bypass, the attacker can extract the vehicle's contract certificate, manipulate AC/DC charging parameters, or deliver a malicious firmware payload via the update channel.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: ISO 15118 is a public standard but OEM-specific certificate chains require research.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires physical access to a charging session (public charger swap or rogue station).

equipment.rationale: Requires a custom EVSE controller or a modified charging station.

threats.name: Tampering
threats.description: Unauthorized modification of data, code, or hardware within the vehicle system. This is the abstract base class for all tampering and integrity-based threats.

threats.name: Runtime Memory/State Tampering
threats.description: Altering variables, system state, or code while it is actively being processed by the ECU in RAM, bypassing static storage protections (e.g., via buffer overflows).

threats.name: Persistent Storage and Configuration/Calibration Tampering
threats.description: Unauthorized logical modification of at-rest configuration files, calibration parameters (e.g., ADAS sensor limits, engine torque curves), routing tables, or OS files stored in NVRAM/EEPROM. Allows an attacker to alter vehicle behavior without needing to exploit or rewrite the actual executable firmware.

elapsedTime.rationale: Changing a config value is much faster than reverse-engineering and patching firmware.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Requires knowing the specific calibration formats or file paths.

threats.name: Static Code Alteration
threats.description: Replacing or modifying the actual software/firmware executables stored on the device. Typically defended by Secure Boot and DM-Verity.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Bypassing hardware-backed trust chains like Secure Boot requires advanced exploitation.

threats.name: Runtime Code/Command Injection
threats.description: Introducing malicious code or unauthorized system commands into a running application by exploiting input validation flaws (e.g., API payloads, malicious diagnostic requests).

threats.name: Hardware Fault Injection (Glitching)
threats.description: Deliberately introducing faults (voltage drops, clock manipulation, EM pulses) to disrupt normal SoC execution, allowing an attacker to skip security checks like Secure Boot or Authentication loops.

equipment.rationale: Requires oscilloscopes, precise timing trigger boards, and modified power supplies.

threats.name: Network Protocol Manipulation / Fuzzing
threats.description: Exploitation of in-vehicle communication protocols (CAN, Ethernet, UDS) by sending malformed or out-of-spec messages to induce crashes, overflow buffers, or force fallback states.

threats.name: Basic Physical Data Tampering (Exposed Pins)
threats.description: Gaining physical access to the ECU's PCB to directly modify memory chips utilizing exposed-pin architectures (TSOP, QFP, DIP), requiring significantly lower technical capabilities than hidden-pad packages.

equipment.rationale: Standard soldering iron, hot air gun, and basic micro-probes are sufficient.

threats.name: Advanced Physical Data Tampering (Hidden Pads)
threats.description: Gaining physical access to the ECU's PCB to directly modify or rewrite memory chips utilizing hidden-pad architectures (BGA, LGA, WSON), bypassing software-level protections.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires advanced soldering and reballing expertise to avoid destroying the IC.

equipment.rationale: Requires BGA rework stations, decapsulation chemicals, or X-ray inspection tools.

threats.name: Remote Code Execution via Telematics/IVI Interface
threats.description: An attacker exploits an unpatched vulnerability in the vehicle's external wireless stack (Bluetooth BLE, Wi-Fi 802.11, or cellular modem firmware) to gain initial unauthenticated code execution on the Telematics Control Unit (TCU) or IVI head unit without any prior vehicle access.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires full wireless stack exploit development (e.g., heap spray over BLE).

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Wi-Fi/BLE requires proximity; cellular is always reachable while vehicle has coverage.

equipment.rationale: Commodity SDR and a Linux laptop are sufficient.

threats.name: Repudiation
threats.description: An entity performs a malicious or unauthorized action and successfully denies having performed it. In an automotive context, this typically involves an attacker masking their tracks by deleting diagnostic logs, or a compromised node successfully denying the origin of a spoofed V2X/backend message. This is the abstract base class for Repudiation.

threats.name: Audit Log Tampering and Evasion
threats.description: An attacker modifies, deletes, or floods Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs), crash data recorders (EDR), or security audit logs to cover their tracks. This ensures the attack cannot be traced, analyzed by the SOC, or legally proven.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Requires knowing where the logs are stored and the UDS routines to clear them.

threats.name: Repudiation of Origin (V2X / Backend)
threats.description: A node transmits a malicious or malformed message (e.g., a fake V2X safety hazard warning or a rogue OTA command) and successfully denies sending it because the communication channel lacks sufficient non-repudiation controls (e.g., missing digital signatures or shared symmetric keys).

threats.name: Information Disclosure
threats.description: Unauthorized exposure of sensitive data, such as personal identifiable information (PII), cryptographic keys, IP, or system state. This is the abstract base class for all disclosure and confidentiality threats.

threats.name: In-Vehicle Network (IVN) Sniffing
threats.description: Passively capturing unencrypted data exchanged within the vehicle (e.g., CAN, LIN, or Automotive Ethernet traffic) to extract sensitive telemetry, user data, or reverse-engineer command structures.

specialistExpertise.rationale: CAN sniffing tools are heavily commoditized.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires physical access to the OBD-II port or an exposed IVN bus wire, or a compromised ECU.

threats.name: External Communications Interception
threats.description: An attacker positions themselves on the vehicle's external communication path (e.g., deploying a rogue Wi-Fi AP, cellular IMSI catcher, or BGP route injection) to intercept data transmitted between the vehicle and external entities. Allows the theft of credentials, session tokens, or PII.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires proximity for Wi-Fi/BLE positioning; carrier-level access for cellular (IMSI catcher).

equipment.rationale: Requires Wi-Fi/Bluetooth sniffers, rogue APs, or SDR base stations.

threats.name: Unintended Disclosure of PII Data
threats.description: Exposure of sensitive user data (location history, contacts, biometrics) due to misconfiguration, excessive debug logging, or insecure HMI interfaces in the Infotainment/Telematics domains.

threats.name: Side Channel Analysis (SCA)
threats.description: Passively exploiting indirect physical leakage — timing variations, power consumption patterns, or electromagnetic emissions — observed during normal cryptographic processing to statistically recover secret key material without physical intervention in circuit operation.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires advanced statistical signal processing (DPA/CPA/TVLA) to isolate key-correlated leakage from measurement noise across thousands of traces.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Knowledge of the cryptographic algorithm and approximate hardware layout is sufficient; exact gate-level implementation not required.

equipment.rationale: Requires high-frequency oscilloscopes (≥1 GSa/s), EM near-field probes, and low-noise amplifiers. Commercially available.

threats.name: Differential Fault Analysis (DFA)
threats.description: Actively inducing precise computational faults in a cryptographic operation — via voltage glitching, clock manipulation, electromagnetic fault injection (EMFI), or laser irradiation — then statistically analyzing the differential between correct and faulty ciphertext outputs to derive the secret key. Unlike SCA, DFA actively disturbs circuit operation rather than passively observing it.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires simultaneous expertise in fault injection hardware, cryptographic differential analysis, and precise timing of fault windows within the cryptographic round schedule.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Exact knowledge of the cryptographic implementation's round timing and target register layout is required to position the fault within the exploitable window.

equipment.rationale: Requires precision fault injection platforms (laser FI workstation, EMFI pulse generators, sub-nanosecond timing controllers). Significantly more specialized than SCA equipment.

threats.name: Advanced Physical Data Extraction (Hidden Pads)
threats.description: Directly reading data from storage devices or memory chips utilizing hidden-pad architectures (BGA, LGA, WSON) using physical access techniques like X-ray alignment, chip-off, or decapsulation to extract firmware and keys.

threats.name: Basic Physical Data Extraction (Exposed Pins)
threats.description: Directly reading data from memory chips utilizing exposed-pin architectures (TSOP, QFP, DIP) using standard in-circuit clips or basic desoldering to extract firmware. Requires significantly less effort than BGA extraction.

equipment.rationale: Standard test clips, logic analyzers, and basic soldering gear are sufficient.

threats.name: OEM Backend Credential / Key Extraction
threats.description: An attacker compromises the OEM's cloud backend through web-application vulnerabilities (SQL injection, insecure object references, credential stuffing against the DevOps pipeline) or insider access to extract cryptographic signing keys, OTA authorization tokens, or fleet-wide symmetric secrets from the backend key management service.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Public API endpoints and OEM web portals are observable.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Backend is always reachable from the internet.

threats.name: Denial of Service (DoS)
threats.description: Disrupting the normal operation of vehicle systems by overwhelming resources, blocking communications, or causing system crashes. This is the abstract base class for all availability threats.

threats.name: In-Vehicle Network (IVN) Bus Flooding
threats.description: Injecting excessive high-priority messages (e.g., CAN frame ID 0x000 flooding, Ethernet broadcast storms) to consume available bandwidth, preventing legitimate safety-critical messages from arriving at their destination.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Simple scripts or off-the-shelf CAN tools can generate bus floods.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires physical access to OBD-II or an exposed network wire.

threats.name: Compute Resource Exhaustion
threats.description: Intentionally consuming excessive system resources on an ECU (CPU cycles, RAM, file descriptors) by exploiting logic flaws, infinite loops, or sending complex continuous inputs, forcing the OS to hang or crash.

threats.name: Wireless RF / GNSS Jamming
threats.description: Emitting powerful RF noise to interfere with and block the vehicle's wireless reception, disabling location-based services (GNSS), cellular telematics, Bluetooth key fobs, or V2X safety communications.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Jamming requires zero expertise; turn the device on and it works.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Attacker must be in physical proximity to the vehicle.

equipment.rationale: GPS/Cellular jammers are cheap and widely available online.

threats.name: Denial of Wallet (Cloud API Exhaustion)
threats.description: An attacker targets the OEM's backend infrastructure by generating massive amounts of automated API requests (e.g., querying vehicle status, requesting OTA updates). This forces the cloud infrastructure to auto-scale, incurring massive financial costs for the OEM.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Public APIs are always available.

threats.name: Denial of Service via Logical Deletion (Bricking)
threats.description: An attacker intentionally deletes critical system files, boot configurations, routing tables, or cryptographic keys from persistent storage. This renders the ECU or vehicle network locally inoperable, resulting in a permanent or semi-permanent denial of service without requiring continuous network flooding.

threats.name: Elevation of Privilege (EoP)
threats.description: An attacker gains higher access rights than intended, allowing them to perform unauthorized actions, access restricted data, or bypass security boundaries. This is the abstract base class for all privilege escalation attacks.

threats.name: OS / Kernel Privilege Escalation
threats.description: Exploiting local vulnerabilities (e.g., buffer overflows, race conditions, use-after-free) within the vehicle's Operating System or Kernel to transition from a restricted user space application to root or administrative access.

threats.name: Hypervisor / TEE Breakout
threats.description: Gaining unauthorized processing capabilities by escaping standard execution environments (Normal World or Guest VM) to access highly protected hardware domains (Secure World, TEE, or Hypervisor root level) running crypto-operations or critical routing.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires extremely advanced exploit development and deep hardware architecture knowledge.

threats.name: Application / Container Sandbox Breakout
threats.description: Escaping application sandboxes (e.g., Android Automotive app permissions) or OCI containers (Docker) running on an HPC node. The attacker elevates from a restricted app context to full host OS access.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Often achievable remotely if an attacker compromises a connected infotainment app.

threats.name: Exploitation of Open Debug Ports (JTAG/UART)
threats.description: Utilizing unprotected or improperly disabled hardware debug channels left open from the development phase to bypass software restrictions, drop into a root shell, or manually alter execution flow.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires physical access to the PCB headers.

threats.name: Elevation of Privilege via Misconfiguration
threats.description: An attacker leverages insecure system configurations, such as default engineering passwords, open Telnet/SSH ports, overly permissive file access controls (DAC), or forgotten debug flags left in production builds, to trivially gain administrative or root access to the system.

elapsedTime.rationale: Exploiting misconfigurations is often instantaneous compared to developing memory corruption exploits.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Can be executed by a layman using automated scanning tools or known default password lists.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Default credentials and open ports are often industry-wide knowledge.

threats.name: Physical ECU / Vehicle Access
threats.description: An attacker gains physical access to vehicle internals (ECU PCB, wiring harness, or OBD-II port), enabling hardware-level attack primitives that cannot be achieved remotely. This is the foundational access condition that roots the physical-access attack chain.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires physical proximity and unobserved access time (e.g., a workshop, overnight parking, or supply chain access).

equipment.rationale: Basic tools (screwdrivers, OBD-II adapter, logic analyzer clips) are sufficient to gain initial physical access to ECU internals.

threats.name: Cross-Domain Lateral Movement
threats.description: An attacker with code execution in the Infotainment or Telematics domain exploits a misconfigured or unfiltered central gateway ECU to inject messages directly onto Powertrain-domain bus segments (e.g., CAN-FD) or escalate to host-OS access on an HPC node bridging both domains.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires deep knowledge of OEM-specific gateway routing tables and firewall rules.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Gateway configuration is OEM-confidential.

threats.name: Reconnaissance
threats.description: The adversary is trying to gather information they can use to plan future operations. Reconnaissance consists of active or passive information gathering to support an attack on the vehicle.

threats.name: Gather Target Information - from Other
threats.description: The adversary gathers information by directly targeting sources that do not involve the vehicle itself, such as OEM supply chains, surface/dark web, engineering manuals, or breach reports.

specialistExpertise.rationale: OSINT and web scraping require basic to intermediate IT skills.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: The goal is to acquire knowledge; starting knowledge is minimal.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Internet-based research has an unlimited window.

threats.name: Gather Target Information - from Vehicle
threats.description: The adversary gathers information by targeting a vehicle's hardware and/or communication environment to extract firmware, map network topologies, or identify component vulnerabilities.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires prolonged physical access to a target vehicle or purchased test-bench ECU.

threats.name: Manipulate Environment
threats.description: The adversary manipulates the physical environment or RF spectrum surrounding a vehicle to confuse its sensors, intercept traffic, or force degraded operational states.

threats.name: Adversarial Machine Learning
threats.description: Supplying deceptive inputs (e.g., physically altered stop signs, adversarial noise patches) to cause vehicle perception systems to misclassify objects or respond inappropriately to road conditions.

elapsedTime.rationale: Generating and validating physical-world adversarial examples is highly time-consuming.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires advanced data science and machine learning expertise to exploit neural network gradients.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: White-box attacks require access to the training weights; black-box requires extensive API querying.

equipment.rationale: Requires heavy GPU compute for model training and evasion testing.

threats.name: Analog Sensor Attacks
threats.description: Disrupting availability or spoofing inputs of analog sensors (e.g., shining a laser into a Lidar receiver, transmitting ultrasonic noise) without directly altering the vehicle's mechanics.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Basic physics applies to all OEMs identically (lasers blind cameras).

threats.name: Downgrade to Insecure Protocols
threats.description: Jamming modern secure frequencies (e.g., 4G/5G, WPA3) to force the vehicle's wireless interfaces to failover to older, vulnerable legacy protocols (e.g., 2G GSM, WEP) to facilitate eavesdropping.

equipment.rationale: Requires SDRs capable of precise frequency isolation and suppression.

threats.name: Jamming or Denial of Service
threats.description: Emitting excessive RF noise to block Wi-Fi, cellular, GPS, or key fobs, preventing the vehicle from communicating with external entities.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Jammers require no expertise to operate.

equipment.rationale: Commercial jammers are inexpensive and easily acquired.

threats.name: Manipulate Communication
threats.description: Intercepting and altering network traffic between the vehicle and a remote server/mobile device (MitM) by exploiting insecure or improperly implemented TLS/encryption schemas.

threats.name: Relay Communications
threats.description: Relaying RF communications to make a trusted device appear physically closer to the vehicle than it is, bypassing distance-bound security assumptions (e.g., Key Fob PKES relay theft).

elapsedTime.rationale: Attack takes seconds once the equipment is in range.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Tools are turnkey and OEM-agnostic for vulnerable LF/RF systems.

threats.name: Rogue Cellular Base Station
threats.description: Setting up a malicious cellular eNodeB/femtocell (IMSI Catcher) to trick the vehicle's telematics unit into connecting, allowing eavesdropping or manipulation of backend communications.

equipment.rationale: IMSI catchers and cellular SDRs are highly specialized hardware.

threats.name: Rogue Wi-Fi Access Point
threats.description: Deploying unauthorized Wi-Fi access points to intercept connections from the vehicle's infotainment system or diagnostic interfaces to execute network-based attacks.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Commoditized tools (e.g., Wi-Fi Pineapple) automate this entirely.

threats.name: Initial Access
threats.description: Adapted from MITRE ATT&CK: The adversary is trying to get into the vehicle network. The initial access tactic represents the vectors adversaries use to gain an initial foothold onto a vehicle.

threats.name: Aftermarket, Customer, or Dealer Equipment
threats.description: Adversaries may use aftermarket, customer, or dealer equipment (e.g., paired smartphones, fleet management dongles, dealer diagnostic tools) as an initial access point into the vehicle.

threats.name: Browser Compromise
threats.description: A browser compromise is when an adversary gains access to a system through a user visiting a website. The user's web browser is targeted for exploitation (e.g., WebKit/V8 engine flaws).

elapsedTime.rationale: Developing reliable zero-click or 1-click browser exploits with sandbox escapes takes months of dedicated effort.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires elite-level proficiency in memory corruption, JIT compilation flaws, and sandbox escapes.

threats.name: Exploit Via Radio Interface
threats.description: The vehicle may be targeted for exploitation through its interface to cellular networks or other radio interfaces via baseband vulnerabilities or malicious SMS parsing.

elapsedTime.rationale: Developing baseband exploits requires substantial time and reverse engineering of proprietary stacks.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Baseband exploitation requires advanced proficiency in real-time operating systems and low-level radio firmware.

equipment.rationale: Requires specialized SDRs and cellular network simulation equipment (eNodeB).

threats.name: Exploit Via Removable Media
threats.description: Adversaries may gain initial access by copying malware to removable media (e.g., USB drive) and taking advantage of automatic parsing of files (e.g., media parsers) when inserted.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires physical access to the vehicle cabin to insert the removable media.

threats.name: Malicious App
threats.description: Malicious applications could be used by adversaries to gain a presence on a vehicle. This includes evading vetting in official stores or distributing via third-party repositories.

threats.name: Phishing
threats.description: Adversaries may send phishing messages to gain access to victim credentials or deploy malicious code. This relies heavily on social engineering the vehicle owner or fleet manager.

elapsedTime.rationale: Phishing campaigns can be spun up very quickly using automated tools.

threats.name: Physical Modification
threats.description: The adversary can make physical modifications to the vehicle's ECUs, wiring harnesses, or swap out legitimate ECUs for malicious ones to gain initial access.

threats.name: Supply Chain Compromise
threats.description: Adversaries may manipulate products or product delivery mechanisms prior to receipt by a final consumer (e.g., compromised OEM software repositories or vendor hardware).

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires compromising a vendor network long before the vehicle reaches the consumer.

threats.name: Execution
threats.description: The adversary is trying to run malicious code. Execution involves infiltrating an ECU's memory space, where it can manipulate data and instructions to carry out unauthorized operations.

threats.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
threats.description: Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters (e.g., Python, Javascript, bash) to execute arbitrary commands, scripts, or binaries within the vehicle's environment.

threats.name: Native API
threats.description: Adversaries may directly interact with the native OS application programming interface (API) to execute behaviors, calling low-level OS services involving hardware/devices, memory, and processes.

threats.name: Abuse Standard Diagnostic Protocol to Temporarily Modify Execution
threats.description: Using standard diagnostic protocols to write directly to RAM (e.g., UDS WriteMemoryByAddress). This alters execution flow or overrides sensitive variables without persistent flashing.

threats.name: Persistence
threats.description: The adversary is trying to maintain their foothold. Persistence consists of techniques that adversaries use to keep access to vehicle systems across restarts, changed credentials, and other interruptions that could cut off their access.

threats.name: Abuse Standard Diagnostic Protocol for Persistence
threats.description: The adversary can attempt to use standard diagnostic protocols to persist their presence on an ECU. For example, UDS' capabilities for firmware updates and 'download-and-execute' can be abused by an adversary to achieve persistence.

specialistExpertise.rationale: While UDS is standardized, bypassing SecurityAccess to write firmware requires intermediate cryptographic or reverse-engineering expertise.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Requires proprietary knowledge of the seed/key algorithm or update signing keys.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Access to the vehicle's UDS access point is required.

equipment.rationale: UDS reader/writers are quite easy and affordable.

threats.name: Disable Software Update
threats.description: The adversary may disable or modify an ECU's software update mechanism so that it cannot be used to restore the device to an uncompromised state.

elapsedTime.rationale: Simply preventing the connection or modifying a config file is relatively fast.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Knowledge of how to prevent the vehicle from communicating with its software update server is required.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Simply understanding how to block basic IP traffic or modify local configs.

threats.name: Modify OS Kernel, Boot Partition, or System Partition
threats.description: If an adversary can escalate privileges, they may place malicious code in the system partition, kernel, or bootloader components where it evades detection and persists after device resets.

elapsedTime.rationale: Finding an exploit for this could take quite some time, particularly if the OS is custom.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires deep knowledge of kernel operation, verified boot chains, and rootkits.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Attackers need to know specifics about the boot chain and kernel layouts.

threats.name: Modify Isolated Execution Environment
threats.description: Using elevated privileges to place malicious code in the ECU's isolated execution environment (TrustZone, HSM, TEE, etc.) where the code can evade detection and monitor overall device behavior.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Escaping the Normal World to inject code into the Secure World/TEE requires elite exploit development.

threats.name: Compromise Cryptographic Security
threats.description: Adversaries compromise cryptographic mechanisms (e.g., exploiting weak algorithms, reusing nonces, or poor implementation) to bypass controls, establish persistent communication, or re-sign malicious firmware.

elapsedTime.rationale: Identifying and exploiting cryptographic flaws requires extensive mathematical and code analysis.

threats.name: Privilege Escalation
threats.description: The adversary is trying to gain higher-level permissions. Privilege Escalation consists of techniques that adversaries use to gain higher-level permissions on an ECU. Common approaches are to take advantage of system weaknesses, misconfigurations, and vulnerabilities.

threats.name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
threats.description: Adversaries may circumvent mechanisms designed to control elevated privileges to gain higher-level permissions, taking advantage of built-in control mechanisms to escalate privileges on a system.

specialistExpertise.rationale: General knowledge of access control systems and the ability to identify the protocols being used is required.

threats.name: Exploit Co-Located Computing Device for Privilege Escalation
threats.description: Adversaries may leverage their access on one computing device (e.g., a Wi-Fi/Bluetooth chip) to gain control of the primary operating system running on a separate chip (SoC) within the same ECU.

elapsedTime.rationale: Exploits for pre-existing commercial wireless devices are often widely documented.

threats.name: Exploit OS Vulnerability
threats.description: A malicious app can exploit unpatched vulnerabilities in the operating system to obtain escalated privileges.

specialistExpertise.rationale: High-level knowledge of operating systems and memory corruption paths is required.

threats.name: Exploit Isolated Execution Environment Vulnerability
threats.description: Exploiting vulnerabilities in code running within an IEE/TEE (TrustZone, HSM, CAAM) to obtain privileges held by the environment, granting access to cryptographic keys or root-level operations.

threats.name: Hardware Fault Injection
threats.description: Attempting fault injection attacks (voltage/clock glitching) to elevate privileges by flipping bits that control authentication states, bypassing secure boot or debug interfaces.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Deep knowledge of embedded hardware architectures and timing analysis is required.

equipment.rationale: Requires specialized glitching hardware like a ChipSHOUTER or FPGA.

threats.name: Process Injection
threats.description: Injecting code into the address space of a separate live process to evade defenses and potentially elevate privileges by masking execution under a highly-privileged legitimate process.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Execution surface is bound to normal runtime operation windows.

threats.name: Reprogram Co-Located Computing Device for Privilege Escalation
threats.description: Elevating privileges by using access on a primary computing device (SoC) to reprogram a secondary microcontroller (e.g., a CAN MCU) on the same ECU.

threats.name: Compromise Cryptographic Security
threats.description: Compromising cryptographic mechanisms to bypass privilege controls, allowing the attacker to elevate their session or extract keys necessary to authenticate as a higher-level user.

threats.name: Defense Evasion
threats.description: The adversary is attempting to bypass or manipulate security controls. This can include bypassing networking filtering, intrusion detection systems (IDS), and code integrity checks to undermine the protection measures in place.

threats.name: Bypass Code Integrity Protections
threats.description: Adversaries may seek to circumvent various code integrity protections (e.g., Secure Boot, signature checks) to install unauthorized firmware or software on the device.

elapsedTime.rationale: Breaking or bypassing hardware-backed integrity checks takes significant time.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires advanced vulnerability research into bootloaders or cryptographic implementations.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Requires knowledge of internal signing mechanisms and trust chains.

threats.name: Bypass Network Filtering
threats.description: Adversaries may disable or modify network filters (firewalls, routing tables) to bypass controls limiting vehicle network usage, enabling lateral movement.

threats.name: Bypass UDS Security Access
threats.description: Adversaries defeat the UDS 'Security Access' (0x27) mechanism by exploiting static seed/keys, weak PRNGs, or brute-forcing short secrets to unlock protected diagnostic routines.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Requires knowing the specific diagnostic IDs and expected seed formats.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires physical access to the OBD-II port or a compromised routing node.

threats.name: Bypass Mandatory Access Control
threats.description: Adversaries defeat MAC mechanisms (SELinux, AppArmor) by finding policy holes or exploiting kernel vulnerabilities, allowing disabled process isolation.

threats.name: Compromise Cryptographic Security
threats.description: Adversaries exploit cryptographic flaws (weak algorithms, timing leaks, poor padding) to bypass authenticity or confidentiality controls.

threats.name: Credential Access
threats.description: The adversary is trying to steal vehicle network credentials. Credential Access consists of techniques for stealing vehicle network credentials like cryptographic tokens, keys, and passwords.

threats.name: Exploit Isolated Execution Environment Vulnerability
threats.description: A malicious app or other vector exploits vulnerabilities in a TEE (TrustZone, HSM) to obtain privileges and access cryptographic keys or sensitive data.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Exploiting the TEE/Secure World requires extremely advanced vulnerability research capabilities.

threats.name: Capture SMS Message
threats.description: A malicious application or intercepted baseband captures sensitive data sent via SMS, including authentication credentials or multi-factor authentication codes.

threats.name: Input Capture
threats.description: Adversaries capture user input via keyloggers or abused accessibility features to obtain credentials or PII from the user.

threats.name: Input Prompt
threats.description: Adversaries mimic legitimate application prompts (e.g., overlay attacks) to trick the user into entering sensitive account credentials or PII.

threats.name: Network Sniffing
threats.description: Adversaries sniff vehicle network traffic (CAN, Ethernet) by placing an interface in promiscuous mode or using physical span ports to capture credentials or telemetry.

threats.name: ECU Credential Dumping
threats.description: Adversaries dump credentials (hashes or clear text passwords) from the operating system or secure enclaves to perform Lateral Movement.

threats.name: Unsecured Credentials
threats.description: Adversaries search compromised file systems for insecurely stored credentials (plaintext files, bash history, unprotected registry keys) to quickly escalate access.

threats.name: URI Hijacking
threats.description: Adversaries register malicious Uniform Resource Identifiers (URIs) to intercept sensitive data (e.g., OAuth tokens) passed between legitimate applications during Single Sign-On.

threats.name: Discovery
threats.description: The adversary is trying to figure out the vehicle environment. Discovery consists of techniques an adversary may use to gain knowledge about a vehicle's systems and its internal network.

threats.name: File and Directory Discovery
threats.description: Adversaries enumerate files and directories or search specific locations of an ECU for sensitive information within a file system using standard OS utilities (ls, find).

elapsedTime.rationale: Directory traversal using built-in OS tools is extremely fast.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Always available once an OS shell is achieved.

threats.name: Location Tracking
threats.description: An adversary could use a malicious or exploited application to surreptitiously track the vehicle's physical location through use of standard operating system APIs.

threats.name: Network Service Scanning
threats.description: Adversaries attempt to get a listing of services running on a vehicle's ECUs using port scans (e.g., nmap) to identify vulnerable remote software.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Port scanning requires basic networking knowledge and uses highly commoditized tools.

threats.name: Process Discovery
threats.description: Adversaries attempt to get information about running processes on a system to understand defensive software or identify vulnerable processes to inject into.

threats.name: Software Discovery
threats.description: Adversaries attempt to get a listing of software and software versions installed on a vehicle to cross-reference against known CVEs.

threats.name: System Information Discovery
threats.description: An adversary attempts to get detailed information about the ECU's operating system and hardware architecture, including patches and hotfixes.

threats.name: System Network Configuration Discovery
threats.description: Adversaries look for details about the network configuration and settings of systems they access (e.g., using arp, ifconfig, or route tables) to facilitate lateral movement.

threats.name: System Network Connections Discovery
threats.description: Adversaries attempt to get a listing of active network connections to or from the compromised system to map out adjacent gateways or backends.

threats.name: Lateral Movement
threats.description: The adversary is trying to move through the vehicle network. Lateral movement consists of techniques that enable an adversary to access and control remote systems on a vehicle network, often traversing boundaries from exposed domains to safety-critical ECUs.

threats.name: Abuse Standard Diagnostic Protocol for Lateral Movement
threats.description: The adversary abuses standard diagnostic protocols (e.g., UDS 'download and execute' or 'write memory by address') over the internal network to gain code execution on adjacent ECUs.

threats.name: Bridge Vehicle Networks
threats.description: The adversary modifies an ECU (like a Central Gateway) that possesses multiple network interfaces to bridge networks that would otherwise be strictly separated (e.g., routing Ethernet payloads onto a proprietary CAN bus).

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires deep knowledge of complex gateway routing logic and network stack exploitation.

knowledgeOfTheItemOrComponent.rationale: Requires strictly confidential knowledge of OEM internal routing tables.

threats.name: Exploit ECU for Lateral Movement
threats.description: Adversaries exploit software vulnerabilities (e.g., buffer overflows) in network services running on adjacent ECUs to execute remote code and hop across the vehicle network.

threats.name: Remote Services
threats.description: Adversaries attempt to log into a service specifically designed to accept remote connections (e.g., Telnet, SSH, VNC) on adjacent ECUs using stolen or default credentials.

threats.name: Reprogram ECU for Lateral Movement
threats.description: The adversary moves laterally by pushing a malicious firmware update over the internal bus to an adjacent ECU, exploiting flaws in the target's update verification mechanism.

threats.name: Compromise Cryptographic Security
threats.description: Adversaries compromise cryptographic mechanisms (e.g., sniffing cleartext, breaking weak algorithms) used in intra-vehicle communication to facilitate lateral movement or bypass internal firewalls.

threats.name: Collection
threats.description: The adversary is trying to gather data of interest to their goal. Collection consists of techniques used to identify and gather information, such as sensitive files, location history, or recordings of in-vehicle audio from a target vehicle prior to exfiltration.

threats.name: Capture SMS Message
threats.description: A malicious application running on the vehicle platform or an asset on an intercepted channel captures sensitive data sent via SMS, including authentication credentials or multi-factor tokens.

elapsedTime.rationale: Intercepting standard API broadcast events takes minimal time once code execution is achieved.

threats.name: Input Capture
threats.description: Adversaries capture raw user inputs to obtain credentials or other sensitive material by implementing rogue input methods, accessibility service keylogging logging, or direct peripheral polling.

elapsedTime.rationale: Polling system input wrappers or deploying Accessibility listeners is instantaneous post-compromise.

threats.name: Network Sniffing
threats.description: Adversaries intercept internal vehicle network traffic (CAN, Ethernet) by setting localized interfaces into promiscuous mode or mapping diagnostics to actively harvest authentication data or telematics.

equipment.rationale: Requires physical installation of capturing hardware or target node shell deployment.

threats.name: Location Tracking
threats.description: Surreptitiously tracking the vehicle's real-time geographic location or extracting historical telemetry through standard location framework APIs or raw GPS data parsing.

threats.name: Abuse Standard Diagnostic Protocol for Collection
threats.description: Abusing 'read' routines within standard diagnostic protocols (e.g., UDS ReadMemoryByAddress) over internal networks to dump memory space containing cryptographic variables or proprietary calibrations.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires connection authorization to the diagnostic bus topology.

threats.name: Capture Camera or Audio
threats.description: Adversaries hook into the vehicle's multimedia drivers or physical hardware capture streams to extract cabin microphone audio, video files, or active safety camera feeds.

threats.name: Data from Local System
threats.description: Adversaries navigate local file systems, databases, or runtime variables via local commands to accumulate target artifacts prior to package offloading.

elapsedTime.rationale: Locating files within standard directories is immediate post-compromise.

threats.name: Network Information Discovery
threats.description: Adversaries query local network chips to map out adjacent devices, active connections, or nearby environmental nodes across Wi-Fi or Bluetooth domains.

threats.name: Screen Capture
threats.description: Adversaries capture screen renderings from the vehicle's primary display outputs to scrape user telemetry, application logs, input variables, or map details.

elapsedTime.rationale: Grabbing standard framebuffers takes minimal execution time once running inside the target process context.

threats.name: Command and Control
threats.description: The adversary is trying to communicate with compromised systems to control them. Command and Control consists of techniques that adversaries may use to communicate with a compromised vehicle and its systems. Adversaries can attempt to mimic normal, expected traffic to avoid detection.

threats.name: Aftermarket, Customer, or Dealer Equipment
threats.description: Adversaries may use aftermarket, customer, or dealer equipment as a command-and-control ingress point. Paired devices (smartphones) or third-party OBD-II accessories (insurance dongles) bridge the gap between the attacker's external infrastructure and the internal vehicle network.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Requires the physical presence of the dongle or the paired status of the mobile device.

threats.name: Cellular Communication
threats.description: The adversary uses the vehicle's legitimate cellular communication system (e.g., Telematics TCU, SMS) in a post-exploitation environment to fulfill their command and control and exfiltration objectives without needing external hardware.

elapsedTime.rationale: If the attacker has a shell on the TCU, routing traffic over the existing cellular connection is trivial.

equipment.rationale: Utilizes the vehicle's built-in modem; no attacker SDR required.

threats.name: Internet Communication
threats.description: The adversary routes C2 traffic over the compromised ECU's established internet connection (e.g., via standard TCP/UDP web protocols) to communicate with external attacker-controlled infrastructure.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Basic reverse-shell and web traffic routing.

threats.name: Receive Only Communication
threats.description: The adversary uses a receive-only communication channel, such as Radio Data System (RDS) or TPMS broadcasts, to send covert asynchronous 'trigger' commands to malware already dormant on the vehicle.

elapsedTime.rationale: Formatting standard RF protocols to contain covert payload triggers requires specific formatting and research.

equipment.rationale: Requires an SDR or specialized transmitter to broadcast the RDS/TPMS signals.

threats.name: Short Range Wireless Communication
threats.description: The adversary uses the vehicle's short-range wireless communication capabilities (Bluetooth, Wi-Fi) to establish a C2 channel with an attacker-controlled laptop or rogue access point in physical proximity to the vehicle.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Attacker must remain within Wi-Fi/Bluetooth range of the vehicle.

threats.name: Standard Cryptographic Protocol
threats.description: Adversaries employ strong encryption algorithms (e.g., AES, TLS) embedded directly within their malware to conceal C2 traffic. This allows the traffic to blend in and bypass vehicle Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) or firewalls without relying on the vehicle's native crypto.

elapsedTime.rationale: Using standard cryptographic libraries inside malware is a standard, automated part of payload generation.

threats.name: Exfiltration
threats.description: The adversary is trying to steal data. Exfiltration consists of techniques that adversaries may use to steal data from the vehicle. Once they've collected data, adversaries often package it to avoid detection while removing it.

threats.name: Aftermarket, Customer, or Dealer Equipment
threats.description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data via connected paired devices (smartphones), third-party OBD-II accessories (insurance dongles), or dealer test tools.

threats.name: Cellular Communication
threats.description: If available, the adversary uses the vehicle's cellular communication system in a post-exploitation environment to exfiltrate collected data to external servers.

elapsedTime.rationale: Routing data over an existing compromised cellular modem is trivial.

threats.name: Internet Communication
threats.description: The adversary uses a compromised ECU's internet connection (TCP/UDP) to exfiltrate data directly to cloud infrastructure.

threats.name: Short Range Wireless Communication
threats.description: The adversary uses the vehicle's short-range wireless communication capabilities, including Bluetooth and Wi-Fi, to offload data to a nearby attacker-controlled receiver.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Receiver must be within physical proximity.

threats.name: Standard Cryptographic Protocol
threats.description: Adversaries employ a known encryption algorithm embedded in their malware to conceal exfiltration traffic, bypassing network-level DLP (Data Loss Prevention) or IDS.

threats.name: Removable Media
threats.description: Adversaries may attempt to exfiltrate data via a physical medium, such as a USB drive connected to the infotainment system.

windowOfOpportunity.rationale: Physical access to the cabin USB ports is required.

threats.name: Physical Access
threats.description: Physical access via a JTAG / debug / serial interface could be used to exfiltrate data -- for example, extracting private keys or firmware via memory remanence/cold boot attacks.

elapsedTime.rationale: Hardware extraction takes significant time for setup, probing, and dumping.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires advanced hardware hacking and reverse engineering.

equipment.rationale: Requires logic analyzers, decapsulation tools, or environmental cooling gear.

threats.name: Affect Vehicle Function
threats.description: The adversary is trying to affect vehicle functions and systems, such as propulsion control, airbag deployment, audio, and displays. This is the ultimate impact tactic in the automotive domain.

threats.name: Adversarial Machine Learning
threats.description: Using adversarial ML techniques to fool models by supplying deceptive input (e.g., altered stop signs) to cause vehicle perception systems to misclassify objects or respond inappropriately.

elapsedTime.rationale: Crafting physical adversarial inputs that survive real-world variables (lighting, angles) takes substantial time.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires deep understanding of neural networks and gradient exploitation.

equipment.rationale: Requires significant GPU compute for training the adversarial noise.

threats.name: Analog Sensor Attacks
threats.description: Using analog sensor attacks to disrupt the availability of vehicle sensors (e.g., shining a laser into a Lidar receiver, transmitting ultrasonic noise).

threats.name: Abuse Standard Diagnostic Protocol for Affecting Vehicle Function
threats.description: The adversary abuses standard diagnostic protocols to actuate vehicle functions, such as using UDS RoutineControl to actuate brakes or WriteMemoryByAddress to alter calibrations.

specialistExpertise.rationale: Requires bypassing UDS security boundaries.

threats.name: CAN Bus Denial of Service
threats.description: Flooding the CAN bus with high-priority messages (e.g., ID 0x000), preventing other ECUs from being able to communicate.

threats.name: Denial of Service on Vehicle Function
threats.description: Performing DoS attacks to disrupt vehicle functionality by overwhelming an ECU with a high volume of requests or exploiting service vulnerabilities to cause a crash.

threats.name: Local Function
threats.description: Using execution access on a targeted ECU to control the local hardware functions actuated directly by that device (e.g., executing code on a body controller to unlock doors).

threats.name: Modify Bus Message
threats.description: An adversary modifies or suppresses messages sent by another ECU on the internal network by winning arbitration or intercepting the bus logically/physically.

threats.name: Unintended Vehicle Network Message
threats.description: Sending unintended messages on the in-vehicle network (e.g., out of sequence, wrong rate, unauthorized parameters) post-escalation to trigger unauthorized physical functions.
